angle.headline: Why Your M&A Diligence Process Finds Everything Except What Kills Deals
angle.thesis: Middle-market owners spend six figures on diligence that audits the past but never diagnoses whether the target can operate without its founder — the single variable that determines post-close performance.
angle.targetPersona: Business owners in growth-through-acquisition mode, particularly those who've completed one deal and are now considering their second, operating with $10M-$75M revenue across the US-LATAM corridor
angle.relevanceToAudience: With USMCA at its six-year review and nearshoring momentum creating acquisition opportunities across the corridor, owners are evaluating targets in Mexico and LATAM — but standard diligence frameworks were built for US-only deals and miss the founder-dependency risk that is magnified across borders and jurisdictions.
talkingPoints: Most M&A diligence answers 'Does this business make money?' but never asks 'Can this business make decisions without the seller in the room?' — the real post-close question.

The failure happens because diligence is structured to audit the past (financials, compliance, IP) rather than diagnose the operating model's ability to execute independently — particularly across time zones and legal systems in corridor deals.

The Machine Test: before you sign the LOI, spend one week asking only this: 'Who makes what decisions when the owner is unavailable?' If the answer is 'it waits' or 'it escalates,' you are buying a job, not a business.
Add one diligence workstream focused exclusively on decision rights, escalation patterns, and information flow — not as compliance check, but as operating model architecture assessment.

peopleAngle.challenge: The seller's leadership team has never operated without the founder's real-time input, and the buyer's integration team lacks the trust or context to make decisions during the 90-day transition window when founder dependency becomes operationally visible.
peopleAngle.framework: Kotter Step 2: Build the Guiding Coalition

entries.fieldReportSummary: [Meta announced this week](https://www.businessinsider.com/meta-layoffs-internal-memo-ai-job-cuts-faq-2026-5) that it will cut 10% of staff on Wednesday, eliminating around 8,000 roles in three waves of notifications timed to 4am local time across regions. angle.headline: Why Your AI Investment Failed Before You Bought It
angle.thesis: Most middle-market businesses are spending on AI tools before equipping employees to use them—not because of poor technology selection, but because the operating model that would support adoption was never built.
angle.targetPersona: Business owners who have attempted or are considering AI implementation, particularly those who have already experienced one failed technology project (ERP, CRM, digital transformation)
angle.relevanceToAudience: The Marsh/Mercer data shows 40% of companies cite spending on AI before employee readiness as their top concern—this is the Technology OS failure pattern these owners have lived before, now repeating at scale with AI. The corridor dimension matters: US-LATAM operators face this across two operating contexts simultaneously.
talkingPoints: The problem: 40% of organizations are deploying AI before their people can actually use it—the same sequence error that killed their last ERP implementation.
The diagnosis: This isn't a training gap—it's an Operating Model OS failure where work processes were never designed to absorb new capability.
The framework: Brunswick's CHRO named it: getting tech ROI requires changing how work actually works, not just adding tools to broken workflows.
The action: Before the next AI purchase, map one complete workflow end-to-end and identify where the handoffs, approvals, and decision rights would need to change for AI to deliver value.

peopleAngle.challenge: Employees must shift from executing defined tasks to redesigning their own workflows—a capability shift that requires them to see and articulate how their work actually happens today before AI can augment it.

entries.fieldReportSummary: A signal for any US owner thinking about a sale within the next 36 months. According to [Middle Market Growth](https://middlemarketgrowth.org/qoeagent-ai-qoe-data-problem/), AI is now eliminating the first 48 hours of a standard quality of earnings engagement on a lower middle market acquisition.
